title: Tolerating Device Failure in Distributed Quantum Computing
abstract: It is desirable that a distributed quantum computer can operate despite the replacement or failure of its constituent components, allowing the reliability of the distributed system to exceed that of its subcomponents. We first show that when quantum error correction is performed over a modular quantum network, quantum devices can be swapped out or replaced, during operation, with minimal impact on logical error rates. We also investigate the ability of the toric and hyperbolic Floquet quantum error correcting codes to protect logical information under low rates of modular node failure. In particular, we show that under the proposed distributed quantum error scheme, the selected codes are able to maintain good logical error suppression during the failure of entire nodes. For catastrophic node failure of probability p/100, we suggest that a distributed toric code would outperform one implemented on a monolithic device below a physical error rate of 0.05%.
summary: This paper proposes a method for performing quantum error correction across a distributed network of quantum processors to tolerate the failure or replacement of individual nodes. It demonstrates that distributed architectures can achieve higher reliability than single, large-scale processors by effectively treating node failures as erasable errors. The study shows that for low physical error rates, a distributed system can significantly outperform a monolithic one even in the presence of catastrophic node loss.
main_problem: How to maintain fault tolerance in distributed quantum computing architectures when individual modules (nodes) experience scheduled downtime or catastrophic, unscheduled failures.
main_result: Distributed quantum error correction (QEC) using toric and hyperbolic Floquet codes can tolerate node failures with minimal overhead, and can even outperform monolithic architectures at physical error rates below 0.05%.
method: Numerical simulations using the Stim package to evaluate logical error rates for distributed toric and Floquet codes under various noise and node-dropout models, utilizing minimum weight perfect matching (MWPM) for decoding.
model_or_system: A modular quantum network of interconnected Quantum Processing Units (QPUs) linked via photonic interfaces. The model includes local circuit noise, non-local noise from entanglement-mediated gates, and a node-failure model (depolarizing channel) representing the loss of entire modules.
key_observables: Logical error rate (P_L) over multiple error correction rounds.
important_parameters: Physical error rate (p), node dropout probability (p_dropout), code distance (d), and number of qubits per node (n_q).
main_assumptions: Node failures are heralded events; the number of lost qubits is small relative to the total code size; and the network can reconfigure via qubit teleportation for scheduled maintenance.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the quantum network layout; Figure 2 illustrates the qubit teleportation process for scheduled node replacement; Figure 3 shows the unscheduled failure process; Figure 6 compares error rates from circuit noise, node failure, and combined models; Figure 7 compares logical error rates for different code distances and types against a monolithic error floor.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of node failure in distributed systems, defines the modular architecture and error models, presents the proposed QEC strategies for both scheduled and unscheduled events, provides simulation results comparing distributed vs. monolithic performance, and discusses scaling and limitations.
why_interesting: It addresses the stability of large-scale quantum networks and provides a framework for managing correlated errors (node loss) which is critical for the development of scalable, modular quantum computing architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: A quantum network to implement a distributed QEC code. A single distributed QEC code is then encoded over the qubits held by the set of QPUs. These devices are intercon- nected by QPIs, between which Bell states can be generated. The planar layout has been chosen for demonstration, and is not a requirement.

Figure 2: Schematic showing how a node (e.g. a QPU) can be swapped out of a quantum network while maintaining a QEC code. (a) For swap-out, data qubits held on Node C are each teleported out onto the unused node, Node E, using shared Bell states. Then, as shown in (b), the network is reconfigured such that Node E has required connectivity to replace Node C in the distributed QEC code.

Figure 3: The process of node swap-out when node failure is unscheduled. (a) A catastrophic failure occurring on Node C. As such, the state of the qubits held at C are corrupted and discarded. Then, as shown in (b), the same number of (data) qubits at Node E are initialised in the maximally mixed state, and Node E is interconnected in place of C.

Figure 4: (a) Logical error rate of toric codes of distance d ∈[4, 6, 8], with nodes of nq = 16 qubits. (b) The same results but for distributed semi-hyperbolic (f = 2) Floquet codes. The swap-out procedure (dashed) has a minimal impact on the logical error rate compared to the quantum memory experiment (solid). The logical error rates were calculated for all k logical observables after r = 32 code rounds.

Figure 5: Logical error rate for the unrotated toric code (d = 6). Results are shown for a monolithic system (green and yellow) and for the code distributed over a set of nq = 16 qubit devices (blue and red). Results under node failure rates of pdropout = 1 × 10−4 show that the distributed QEC code maintains error correction capabilities, below the analytical floor of a monolithic device (dashed black).
